items.q: Can binary search be used on an unsorted list?
items.correct: No - binary search relies on the list being sorted so it can discard half the items at each comparison; on unsorted data it can miss the target.
items.misconception: Yes - binary search works on any list because it checks the middle item.
items.random_wrong: Yes - binary search sorts the list automatically as it searches.
items.why: Students learn the halving mechanic but forget its precondition. Discarding half the list is only safe if order guarantees which half the target must be in.

items.q: Does O(n^2) mean an algorithm is exactly twice as slow as an O(n) one?
items.correct: No - Big-O describes growth rate: doubling the input roughly quadruples the work for O(n^2) but only doubles it for O(n); the gap widens with input size.
items.misconception: Yes - the exponent 2 means it always takes twice as long.
items.random_wrong: Yes - O(n^2) algorithms always take two seconds per item.
items.why: Students read Big-O as a fixed multiplier. It is a function of input size, so the difference between O(n) and O(n^2) is small for tiny inputs and enormous for large ones.

items.q: Does depth-first search always find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
items.correct: No - DFS plunges down one path and may reach the target by a long route; breadth-first search, exploring level by level, finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
items.misconception: Yes - any traversal that reaches the target finds the shortest route.
items.random_wrong: Yes - DFS is shortest because stacks are faster than queues.
items.why: Reaching a node is confused with reaching it optimally. BFS visits all nodes at distance k before any at distance k+1, which is exactly why its first arrival is shortest.

items.q: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm is not guaranteed to work correctly when the graph contains:
items.why: It's Negative edge weights. “More than ten vertices” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which data structures do depth-first and breadth-first traversal typically use?
items.correct: DFS uses a stack; BFS uses a queue
items.misconception: DFS uses a queue; BFS uses a stack
items.why: Correct answer: DFS uses a stack; BFS uses a queue. “DFS uses a queue; BFS uses a stack” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Binary search can only be applied to data that is:
items.why: Correct answer: Sorted into order. “Stored in a linked list” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The worst-case time complexity of merge sort is:
items.why: It's O(n log n). “O(n^2)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Binary search repeatedly halves the search space. What is log2(1024), the number of halvings needed to reduce 1024 items to 1?
items.why: The real figure is 10 halvings. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
